---
title: "Setup: How to setup and run the tutorial"
---

## Download notebooks

Download notebooks from the right sidebar.
Select `Colab` if you are using [Google Colab](#google-colab).

## Computational environments

### JupyterHub at UHH

1. Open <https://code.min.uni-hamburg.de/>
1. Select `File` > `New Launcher`
1. At the bottom, select `Terminal`
1. In the Terminal, enter:

    ```bash
    git clone {{< var project.source >}}
    cd {{< var project.name >}}
    make install
    ```

1. From the sidebar on the left, you can now open the tutorial notebook in the `{{< var project.name >}}` folder:

    - `tutorial.ipynb`

1. Paste the installation command into the first cell of the notebook:

    ```bash
    source .venv/bin/activate
    ```

### Google Colab

::: {.callout-warning title="Notebook in Google Colab not working"}
Unfortunately, executing the notebook in Google Colab currently does not work.
Please copy-paste the commands into the Terminal in Google Colab.
:::

1. Open <https://colab.research.google.com/>
1. Sign in with a Google account
1. Select `File` > `Open notebook`
1. Select `Upload` and open or drag the notebook file
1. Paste the installation command into the first cell of the notebook:

{{< include _colab_install.md >}}

### Docker Container

The tutorial can also be run inside a Docker container that has DataLad and all its dependencies pre-installed.
This requires [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) to be installed on your system.

1. Clone the repository and navigate into it:

    ```bash
    git clone {{< var project.source >}}
    cd {{< var project.name >}}
    ```

1. Build the DataLad Docker image:

    ```bash
    make build-datalad
    ```

    Or directly with Docker:

    ```bash
    docker build -f Dockerfile.datalad -t lnnrtwttkhn/rdm-datalad-datalad .
    ```

1. Start an interactive shell inside the container:

    ```bash
    make run-datalad
    ```

    Or directly with Docker:

    ```bash
    docker run --rm -it lnnrtwttkhn/rdm-datalad-datalad
    ```

    This drops you into a Bash shell with DataLad, git-annex, and all dependencies ready to use.

1. Verify the installation inside the container:

    ```bash
    datalad --version
    git annex version
    ```

1. You can now copy-paste all commands from the tutorial directly into this shell.

::: {.callout-note}
The container image is built from `Dockerfile.datalad` in the repository root.
Each `make run-datalad` call starts a fresh, temporary container (`--rm`).
To persist data between sessions, mount a local directory:

```bash
docker run --rm -it -v "$(pwd)":/work lnnrtwttkhn/rdm-datalad-datalad
```
:::

### Terminal

1. Open a Terminal and copy-paste all commands in the tutorial

## Connecting to a remote repository hosting service

### Authentication

To connect your DataLad repository to a remote repository, you need to establish an authentication method.
Authentication verifies your identity before granting access to a remote repository — similar to showing your ID before entering a restricted area.
It ensures that only authorized people or programs can read from or write to the repository.
Different platforms support different authentication methods.
We introduce the most common ones below, separately for each platform.

### Secure Shell Protocol (SSH)

The Secure Shell Protocol (SSH) is a cryptographic network protocol for operating network services securely over an unsecured network.
Its most notable applications are remote login and command-line execution.
You can read and write data in remote repositories like GitHub and GitLab using SSH.
For more details on SSH, see [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secure_Shell).

::: {#nte-ssh .callout-note title="How does SSH work: The key and padlock analogy." collapse="true"}
When you set up SSH, you generate a **key pair**: the *private key* stays on your device, while the *public key* is copied to the servers you want access to.

Think of your **public key as a padlock**.
You can make lots of copies of this padlock and distribute them to different places - servers, computers, or anything you want to secure (just like sharing a padlock to secure different lockers). These places install your padlock, but no one can open it because they don't have your key.

Your **private key is the actual key** that opens those padlocks.
You keep it safe and never share it.
As long as your key stays secure, it doesn't matter how many padlocks you've distributed.

!"Understanding public key private key concepts" by [Blake Smith](https://blakesmith.me/) (License: Unknown; [Source on GitHub](https://blakesmith.me/2010/02/08/understanding-public-key-private-key-concepts.html))](https://blakesmith.me/images/public_private_many.png){#fig-ssh-key-public-private width="60%"}

When you try to connect to a server, SSH checks if the padlock (public key) on that server matches your key.
If they match, the door opens.

This system ensures that only someone with the private key (you) can unlock what's protected by the padlocks (public keys).
For extra security, you can protect your private key with an additional password.
Even if someone steals your key, they still need the combination (password) to use it.
For additional discussion of using an additional password for a private SSH key, see @tip-ssh-password.

:::

We provide basic instructions that are applicable to many remote repositories below.
For more detailed instructions, see the [GitHub Documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/about-ssh).
The most basic SSH key setup works like this:

1. Generating a new SSH key.
1. Copying the SSH key to your clipboard.
1. Adding the SSH key to your GitHub or GitLab account.

#### Generating a new SSH key

1. Open the Command Line.
2. Run the command below, replacing the example email address with your own.

```bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com" # <1>
```
1. Replace `your_email@example.com` with your email address.

This will result in an output similar to this:

```console
Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Enter file in which to save the key (/Users/user/.ssh/id_ed25519): 
```

3. When asked to "Enter a file in which to save the key", press {{< kbd Enter >}} to accept the default location.

::: {.callout-caution title="SSH key already exists?" collapse="true"}

If the SSH key already exists, you will get an output similar to this:

```console
/Users/user/.ssh/id_ed25519 already exists.
Overwrite (y/n)? n
```

If you don't want to overwrite the existing key, type `n` and hit {{< kbd Enter >}}.
You can now use the existing key and move on with the next step (adding the SSH key to your remote repository hosting service).

:::

4. When prompted, enter a secure passphrase.
If you do not want to use a passphrase, press {{< kbd Enter >}} twice to skip.

```console
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase): [Type a passphrase]
Enter same passphrase again: [Type passphrase again]
```

::: {#tip-ssh-password .callout-tip title="Do I have to provide a password for my SSH key?" collapse="true"}
If someone gains access to your computer, they can use your SSH keys to access all systems that rely on those keys.
For extra security, you can protect your private key with a passphrase.
Even if someone steals your private key, they still need the passphrase to use it.
Adding a passphrase to your private SSH key therefore makes it significantly more secure.

To avoid typing the passphrase each time you connect, you can securely store it in an SSH agent.
For more details, see the [GitHub documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/working-with-ssh-key-passphrases).
:::

::: {#nte-t-flag .callout-note title="What does the `-t` flag in the `ssh-keygen` command do?" collapse="true"}
The `-t` flag in the `ssh-keygen` command specifies the **type of key** to generate.
Common options include `rsa`, `ecdsa`, and `ed25519`.
For example, `-t ed25519` generates a modern and secure Ed25519 key, which is recommended for most users.
This flag ensures you're using the desired encryption algorithm when creating your SSH key.
:::

After entering (or skipping) a passphrase, your terminal output should look similar to this: 

```console
Your identification has been saved in /Users/user/.ssh/id_ed25519
Your public key has been saved in /Users/user/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
The key fingerprint is:
SHA256: A2C4D8E9F9B3B2A4157C8D9E1FBB4D567543F2C9C4E6A3D422D8F7A1B3E5C6D0 user@email.com
The key's randomart image is:
+--[ED25519 256]--+
|               . |
|              +  |
|             Eo. |
|    .         =+.|
|   . .. S    = =o|
|. . ...+ .  . B +|
|.+ = .+ *  . +.*o|
|..=..+.= ..   Bo=|
| .o.o..   ..  .++|
+----[SHA256]-----+
```

#### Copying the public SSH key to your clipboard

Now copy the SSH public key to your clipboard.
The following command will paste the contents of the `id_ed25519.pub` file to your Terminal (assuming your SSH key is in the default location).

```bash
clip < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
```

If the `clip` command isn't working, find the hidden `.ssh` folder in your home directory, open the `id_ed25519.pub` file in your favorite text editor, and copy the contents to your clipboard.

## {{< fa brands linux >}} Linux

```bash
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
```

Then select and copy the contents of the `id_ed25519.pub` file displayed in the terminal to your clipboard.

Alternatively, find the hidden `.ssh` folder in your home directory, open the `id_ed25519.pub` file in your favorite text editor, and copy the contents to your clipboard.

:::

#### Adding the SSH key to your account

Finally, add the public SSH key to your account on the remote repository hosting service.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## {{< fa brands github >}} GitHub

1. In the upper-right corner of any page on GitHub, click your profile photo, then click **Settings**.
1. In the "Access" section of the sidebar, click **SSH and GPG keys**.
1. Click **New SSH key** or **Add SSH key**.
1. In the "Title" field, add a descriptive label for the new key.
For example, if you're using a personal laptop, you might call it "Personal laptop".
1. Choose the type of key, either authentication or signing.
Usually, you want to select "authentication" here.
1. In the "Key" field, paste your public key.
1. Click **Add SSH key**.
1. If prompted, confirm access to your GitHub account.

:::
